tweets.text: I am not a strong believer in the stock of holdings impacting prices but do believe in the flow creating temporary dislocations.  However the expectations of "flow". Specifically the big print and bills usage  And actual flow including  RMO balance sheet injections and GSE short term financed mortgage purchase recently are a long list of suppressive flows which continues to suppress term premiums

thread_tweets.text: I was on the convertible bond trading desk and our mandate was all convertible preferreds, bonds, and warrants. At the time merger consideration form shareholders was in a renaissance in which new things like warrants, valuation rights, and debt securities were added as consideration on top of the more traditional shares and cash.  The RJR deal consideration was perhaps the most complex in history and the deal was the largest LBO.  It got broad attention and the warrants caught my eye.

tweets.text:
Is there legally binding documentation in place around the perpetual inference? 

There’s certain criteria around recognition of VVV as a liability on the corporate balance sheet

If the VVV token is a foot note as a contingent liability then that would be clear as day for me personally - keen to see the corporate financials on this one

thread_tweets.text:
Constructive obligation, but some serious valuation experts needed to calculate an appropriate “reliable” measurement for the liability 

Mgmt. also have discretion to discontinue the perpetual inference at any point?

I like what the Venice team have built but loopholes need to be plugged
thread_tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text:
It’s logical what @hosseeb says re: VVV as a liability but I’d be keen to see the financial statements as recognising a perpetual liability on the balance sheet would be insanely challenging to measure reliably 

My concern would be that at some point in the future the perpetual inference offered could be renounced which would send the token to zero in favour of equity holders

tweets.text:
Venice is business, not a protocol. It's always been a business from day one. The overseas nonprofit foundation thing is for protocols, it makes no sense for a business.

And Venice did not bootstrap their business by selling tokens to speculators. They haven't sold any tokens, they've been net buyers of the token.

thread_tweets.text:

thread_tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text: @BaggerWalmart @AlgodTrading The founders and the company itself did not sell VVV. Individual employees sold some of their VVV, but that's not "bootstrapping a businesss."
thread_tweets.full_text: @BaggerWalmart @AlgodTrading The founders and the company itself did not sell VVV. Individual employees sold some of their VVV, but that's not "bootstrapping a businesss."

thread_tweets.text:
At the time of the airdrop the company had like 2 engineers. Venice did not have significant capital expenditures until ~1yr ago when they started to hit PMF and scale.

This is pretty reaching.

When I hear "bootstrapped by selling tokens to speculators," I don't think "they paid their first couple of engineers a little less than they'd otherwise have to."
thread_tweets.full_text:
At the time of the airdrop the company had like 2 engineers. Venice did not have significant capital expenditures until ~1yr ago when they started to hit PMF and scale.

This is pretty reaching.

When I hear "bootstrapped by selling tokens to speculators," I don't think "they paid their first couple of engineers a little less than they'd otherwise have to."

thread_tweets.text:
No one's denying that VVV has been used for growth. The reason to have the token is for the business to grow, obviously.

But calling it bootstrapped by "tokens paid for by speculators" is obviously not a good description of what happened here, and I think anyone reading this can see that. 

Because that happens all the time in crypto, as you and I both know, and that's just not a good description of Venice.

tweets.text:
Nikkei - $JD E-Commerce Giant CEO warns "Robots Will Replace 700,000 Delivery Workers". 

Per FT's earlier piece last month: The 700,000 delivery personnel working for the firm will be replaced by robots, highlighting how rapid automation has become a new threat to China's already severe job market.

Liu Qiangdong, revealed that the company has signed contracts with approximately 120 schools to retrain its delivery workers, enabling them to transition into new roles such as robot repair and maintenance...

"Beijing has started to track AI’s hit to jobs as a national priority."

This follows other corporate strategy reports like $AMZN, of avoiding 600,000 future hires with robots.  

But it's very interesting to see the shift in worker roles to robot support. Maybe $DASH to $UBER to $MELI, we'll all start to see this rollout following China. 

There's a lot of retail disbelief over humanoid/robotics commercialization. But feels like the direction industry is heading down is pretty clear.

thread_tweets.text:
No, it’s the same if not higher. Markets are closed and if there’s no new developments, then I have nothing new to add.

$SIVE is personally one of my largest holdings and I’ve posted all my thoughts on their hyperscaler supplier relationships. So I’m just waiting for volume ramps and company execution and ignoring a lot of noise. 

The convertible notes is not new information and was likely priced in for awhile. Having it executed now is not a positive thing like others are framing, since Bootstrap Europe is a venture debt fund.

There’s likely instituonal swaps happening given oversubscribed instituonal demand around these levels. $140m is not a big amount in terms of institutional flows.

thread_tweets.text:
Agree, it's beautiful, top tier fablemaxxing! :)
I think with every new model tier there is something new that qualitatively leaps and surprises, for Fable so far these threejs envs seem to be up there. The bear thing is such a weird little detail to have there, and the fish actually struggles in its mouth. How does an LLM even know all these things from just internet, and then have the ability to convert that understanding into xyz coordinates, meshes, transforms, animations, effects, interactivity and mini stories? Amazing to think about what +1, +2, +3 model tiers might be able to create.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Agree, it's beautiful, top tier fablemaxxing! :)
I think with every new model tier there is something new that qualitatively leaps and surprises, for Fable so far these threejs envs seem to be up there. The bear thing is such a weird little detail to have there, and the fish actually struggles in its mouth. How does an LLM even know all these things from just internet, and then have the ability to convert that understanding into xyz coordinates, meshes, transforms, animations, effects, interactivity and mini stories? Amazing to think about what +1, +2, +3 model tiers might be able to create.

tweets.text:
honestly think this is a great explainer

what I think protocols (in general) could do better to avoid pissing tokenholders off:

be 100% transparent about deals like these, incl price

in an ideal world, you’d mirror such deals to allow existing tokenholders to participate on the same terms pro-rata … but in the real world that’s not easy
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: On tokens vs equity, Venice, and $VVV https://t.co/o2CBN8yX75

We had a first real week of rally after a long long time

> Onchain inference performed the best
> Other DeAI sectors came in second

In this week's the After Hour, we're diving into all things inference + Venice latest fundraising round + Dolphin's rise + c0mpute sharded inference + and a lot more

Link below ↓

are all getting the biggest validation this week

BUT..... investable opportunities remain quite limited — high revenue DeAI projects accrue value to equity and only use token as incentives
